one_line_takeaway: PenForge shows that dynamically constructing attack-type-specific LLM agents on the fly — guided by an upfront reconnaissance phase — triples zero-day exploit success on CVE-Bench (30% vs 10%), though absolute success stays modest and tool misuse remains the main bottleneck.
key_finding: In the zero-day setting PenForge reaches success@5 = 30.0% (12/40), a 3x improvement over the best baseline (T-Agent at 10.0%, 4/40; AutoGPT also 10.0% 4/40; Cy-Agent 2.5% 1/40). On success@1 it scores 20.5%, more than double T-Agent's 8.0% (Cy-Agent 1.0%, AutoGPT 3.0%). Among its 12 successful exploits, 'unauthorized administrator login' and 'outbound service' each account for 33.3% (4/12), because these expose explicit high-value endpoints (login interfaces, outbound-request APIs) that the reconnaissance phase surfaces. The dominant failure mode is tool misuse (wrong tool or wrong parameters), indicating the Meta-Planner supplies good context but agent-level execution needs more robust tool integration. Notably PenForge sometimes exploits a CVE via a different attack type than CVE-Bench annotates (e.g., CVE-2024-37831 labeled 'Database Access' but exploited via 'Unauthorized Administrator Login'; CVE-2024-4443 labeled 'Database Access' but exploited via 'Database Modification'), exposing a benchmark-labeling limitation.
core_contribution: PenForge is an LLM-agent framework for automated web-application penetration testing whose distinguishing idea is constructing attack-type-specific expert agents ON THE FLY during testing, rather than using a single generic agent (as in PentestGPT/Cy-Agent) or a fixed set of hand-crafted expert agents prepared beforehand (as in the T-Agent line of work, which the authors position as the prior state of the art). The novelty is a two-phase Meta-Planner orchestrator: Phase I (Target Reconnaissance) probes the target to map endpoints/interfaces and rank likely attack types; Phase II (Sequential Attack Attempts) invokes an Expert Agent Constructor that prompts an LLM to generate an AutoGPT execution script — a task prompt enriched with target-specific context (attack endpoints), a role summary, best practices, and constraints — to instantiate a specialized agent for the top-ranked attack, retrying with the next candidate agent on failure. This is an ICSE-NIER (New Ideas / Emerging Results) short paper (5 pages) presenting an early-stage result.
threat_model: The framework is a defensive/offensive-security tool: it automates the role of a penetration tester to discover and exploit vulnerabilities in web applications before real adversaries do. Evaluation targets the zero-day setting of CVE-Bench, where the agent is given ONLY the target URL plus a list of 8 candidate attack types (Denial of Service, File Access, File Creation, Database Modification, Database Access, Unauthorized Administrator Login, Privilege Escalation, Outbound Service) — with no application name, attack entry point, or ground-truth attack type. This is the paper's own stated 'more realistic and more challenging' threat scenario, contrasted with the one-day setting where attack type and vulnerable endpoint are supplied.
limitations: (1) Modest absolute performance — even the best result exploits only 12 of 40 targets (30%). (2) Small benchmark: a single 40-case suite (CVE-Bench), and CVE-Bench maps each target to a single reproduced CVE, which the authors themselves call unrealistic. (3) Baseline numbers for Cy-Agent/AutoGPT/T-Agent are taken from CVE-Bench rather than re-run by the authors. (4) The KnowledgeRetriever is a 'simple' generic Perplexity-API retriever, not pentest-specific; tool misuse is the leading cause of failures. (5) As a NIER short paper it lacks ablations isolating the contribution of each component (reconnaissance vs. on-the-fly construction), and no statistical significance/variance is reported beyond the n=5 success@k estimates. (6) No cost/latency accounting. (7) Ethical/dual-use and human-in-the-loop safeguards are named only as future work.

datasets_used: CVE-Bench: 40 real-world web-application vulnerabilities drawn from CVEs/NVD, each with CVSS >= 9.0 (Critical), spanning 8 attack types; evaluated in zero-day mode
quantitative_results: Successful-exploit distribution: Unauthorized administrator login 33.3% (4/12), Outbound service 33.3% (4/12), File Access 16.7%, Database modification 8.3%, Denial of service 8.3%
Setup: Claude-3.7-Sonnet-20250219 backbone, temperature 0.5, AutoGPT max 30 iterations, n=5 attempts per target
baselines_compared: T-Agent (manually constructed attack-type-specific expert agents; prior state of the art)
